bytes.topic: Project Setup & Config — zero-config vs explicit config, config file structure
bytes.title: Turbopack Config — Zero-Config Defaults vs. Explicit turbo.config.ts
bytes.body:
Turbopack ships zero-config inside Next.js: run `next dev --turbopack` and it resolves aliases, handles CSS Modules, and tree-shakes without a single config line. That default pipeline covers ~95% of apps.

When you need to step outside that default, you reach for `turbo.config.ts` (or the `experimental.turbo` key inside `next.config.ts` for Next.js 14+). The two levers you'll actually touch are `resolveAlias` — a direct replacement for webpack's `resolve.alias` — and `rules`, which maps file extensions to custom loaders. Turbopack rules use the `loaders` array, each entry specifying a `loader` package and `options`, mirroring webpack loader syntax but evaluated by Turbopack's Rust core, not Node.

Critical distinction: Turbopack does **not** read `webpack.config.js`. Any custom loader you relied on must be re-declared under `rules`. Not all webpack loaders are compatible — Turbopack only supports a subset of the webpack loader API. Check the official compatibility list before migrating. For monorepos, `turbo.json` at the workspace root controls task pipelines (build, lint, test), which is a separate concern from the bundler config.

bytes.topic: Dev Server — HMR, proxy setup, HTTPS in dev
bytes.title: Turbopack Dev Server — HMR, Proxy Setup, and HTTPS in Dev
bytes.body:
Turbopack's dev server ships with React Fast Refresh-backed HMR out of the box. Unlike webpack's full-module replacement, Turbopack tracks fine-grained module boundaries and only re-evaluates the exact changed module plus its direct dependents — keeping HMR updates sub-50ms even in large apps. No manual `hot.accept()` wiring needed for React components.

For proxying API requests, configure `rewrites` in `next.config.ts`. Turbopack respects Next.js rewrites natively — no separate `devServer.proxy` block like webpack requires. Add a `rewrites` async function and Turbopack picks it up without a server restart.

For HTTPS in dev, pass `--experimental-https` to `next dev --turbopack`. This auto-generates a self-signed cert via `mkcert` and serves over `https://localhost:3000`. Pair it with `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` if your fetch calls hit internal services that reject self-signed certs. Turbopack does not yet support custom cert paths via config — the `--experimental-https` flag is the only supported path as of Next.js 15.

bytes.topic: Code Splitting — manual chunks, dynamic imports, route-based splitting
bytes.title: Turbopack Code Splitting — Dynamic Imports, Manual Chunks, and Route-Based Boundaries
bytes.body:
Turbopack performs automatic route-based splitting in Next.js 13+ app directory — each `page.tsx` becomes its own chunk boundary without configuration. But you control granularity further with dynamic imports via `next/dynamic`, which Turbopack traces statically at build time rather than deferring to runtime like webpack.

Unlike webpack's `optimization.splitChunks`, Turbopack has no manual chunk configuration API yet — chunk splitting is graph-driven, inferred from your import graph. This means co-located heavy dependencies (e.g. a charting lib used in one route) are automatically isolated if they aren't shared across entry points.

For explicit splitting, `next/dynamic` with `ssr: false` tells Turbopack to exclude the module from the server graph entirely, shrinking your server bundle. Pair it with `loading` for streaming UX. Turbopack also respects `React.lazy` + `Suspense` for client-only split points, but `next/dynamic` gives you the `ssr` escape hatch `React.lazy` doesn't.

Avoid barrel files (`index.ts` re-exporting everything) — Turbopack's tree-shaking operates per-module, and barrels collapse chunk boundaries by merging import graphs.

bytes.topic: Tree Shaking — how it works, why side effects matter, marking packages as pure
bytes.title: Turbopack Tree Shaking — Why sideEffects Is the Flag That Actually Matters
bytes.body:
Turbopack performs tree shaking at the module graph level using its Rust-based static analyzer, not at emit time like webpack. When it encounters an ESM import, it traces export bindings through its internal EcmascriptChunkItem graph and prunes exports with no live references before evaluation.

The sideEffects field in package.json is the critical signal. If absent or true, Turbopack must retain every imported module file even if no exports are used — any file might mutate globals, patch prototypes, or register effects. Set "sideEffects": false to declare the entire package pure; use an array (["**/*.css", "src/polyfills.ts"]) to whitelist only files with real effects.

For monorepo internal packages, add "sideEffects": false to each package's package.json. Turbopack will then eliminate barrel-imported but unused modules entirely. Without it, a barrel like export * from './components' forces every component module into the bundle regardless of usage.

CSS imports are always side-effectful — list them explicitly or Turbopack may silently drop your styles.

bytes.topic: Bundle Analysis — visualizing size, finding bloat, setting size budgets in CI
bytes.title: Turbopack Bundle Analysis — Visualizing Size, Finding Bloat, and Enforcing CI Budgets
bytes.body:
Turbopack doesn't support `@next/bundle-analyzer` — that package requires webpack mode. To get a treemap visualization, drop the turbopack flag temporarily: `ANALYZE=true next build` (webpack) gives you the familiar interactive treemap via `@next/bundle-analyzer`. This is a deliberate workflow split: use webpack for periodic audit, Turbopack for fast daily iteration.

For continuous size enforcement in CI, Turbopack emits client chunks to `.next/static/chunks/` after every `next build --turbopack`. Script against that directory to catch regressions before merge. The `turbopack` config key in `next.config.ts` (promoted from `experimental.turbo` in Next.js 15) handles resolver aliases and custom loaders — budget enforcement is intentionally a CI-layer concern, not a bundler config concern.

To find bloat without a treemap, check `.next/build-manifest.json` for which pages pull in which chunks, then cross-reference chunk filenames with the emitted files. Large shared chunks under `chunks/` signal unintentional module coalescing across routes. Run the script below as a `check:size` step after `next build` in your GitHub Actions workflow.

bytes.topic: Asset Handling — images, fonts, SVGs, hashing for cache busting
bytes.title: Turbopack Asset Handling — Images, Fonts, SVGs, and Content Hashing
bytes.body:
Turbopack processes static assets through its native asset pipeline, distinct from webpack's `file-loader` or `url-loader` approach. Import an image directly and Turbopack resolves it to a content-hashed URL automatically — no loader config required.

For images, Turbopack inlines assets below 8KB as base64 data URIs by default and emits larger files with a `[contenthash]` fingerprint (e.g., `logo.a3f9c1d2.png`), enabling aggressive long-term caching without manual cache-busting.

SVGs imported as modules are treated as raw strings unless you configure a custom transform. In Next.js 15+ with Turbopack enabled, `@svgr/webpack` is replaced by the built-in `experimental.turbo.rules` — map `*.svg` to a custom Turbopack loader that returns a React component.

Fonts referenced via CSS `@font-face` or Next.js `next/font` are tracked as module graph edges. Turbopack hashes font files independently, so a font update busts only its own cache entry — not the entire CSS bundle. Unlike webpack, Turbopack never re-emits unchanged assets, making incremental builds with many static files significantly faster.

bytes.topic: Environment Variables — .env files, build-time vs runtime, define plugin
bytes.title: Turbopack Environment Variables — Build-Time Injection vs Runtime Access
bytes.body:
Turbopack handles environment variables differently from webpack. Variables prefixed with NEXT_PUBLIC_ are inlined at build time — Turbopack statically replaces every reference with the literal string value during compilation, making them immutable post-build. There's no webpack DefinePlugin equivalent you configure manually; Turbopack's define behavior is built into the Next.js layer via next.config.ts using the env key for custom build-time constants.

For runtime variables (no NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix), Turbopack never bundles them into client code — they remain server-side only, accessible via process.env on the Node.js runtime. Attempting to read a non-prefixed variable in a Client Component returns undefined silently.

To inject arbitrary build-time constants (like feature flags or API versions), use next.config.ts env field. Turbopack reads this during its module graph traversal and performs dead-code elimination on branches that resolve to false constants — a meaningful tree-shaking win over runtime checks. Always validate required env vars at startup using a Zod schema rather than scattering process.env reads across modules.

bytes.topic: Source Maps — types, trade-offs in production
bytes.title: Turbopack Source Maps — Types, Trade-offs, and What to Ship in Production
bytes.body:
Turbopack generates source maps incrementally — only reprocessing changed modules — so high-fidelity maps add minimal overhead during development. Production is a different calculus.

Next.js exposes a single toggle for shipping maps to the browser: `productionBrowserSourceMaps: true` in `next.config.ts`. With this enabled, `.map` files are emitted alongside JS bundles and referenced via `//# sourceMappingURL`. Anyone with DevTools can read your original source. For internal tooling, that's acceptable. For competitive or IP-sensitive products, it's a liability.

The safer pattern: generate maps, upload them to your error tracker, then exclude them from your CDN deploy. `@sentry/nextjs` handles this via `withSentryConfig` — it uploads maps to Sentry's API then deletes them post-build. Note: Turbopack plugin support in `withSentryConfig` is still rolling out as of Next.js 15.3, so verify your Sentry SDK version supports it.

Unlike webpack, Turbopack doesn't expose a `devtool` enum (`cheap-module-source-map`, `eval-source-map`, etc.). Source map fidelity is controlled at the Next.js config level, not per-loader. This removes the escape hatch of trading map quality for build speed — but Turbopack's Rust core makes that trade-off largely irrelevant in dev anyway.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — transpile-only vs type-check, speed vs safety trade-off
bytes.title: Turbopack — Transpile-Only vs Type-Checking: The Speed-Safety Trade-Off
bytes.body:
Turbopack, like esbuild before it, strips TypeScript types at build time without running `tsc`. This is transpile-only mode — it removes annotations and emits JS at near-instant speeds, but never catches type errors. In Next.js 15+ with Turbopack as the default dev bundler, this is exactly what happens: `next dev --turbo` gives you sub-100ms HMR because type-checking is simply not part of the pipeline.

The implication: a component can have `string` where you meant `number`, and Turbopack will happily bundle and serve it. Your dev server stays fast; your runtime blows up.

The pragmatic fix is a parallel type-check process. Run `tsc --noEmit --watch` in a second terminal alongside `next dev --turbo`. You get Turbopack's speed for bundling and incremental React updates, while tsc catches contract violations independently. In CI, gate merges on `tsc --noEmit` — never on the Turbopack build output itself. This two-track model is the production-safe pattern: Turbopack owns speed, tsc owns correctness.

bytes.topic: CSS Processing — PostCSS, CSS Modules, preprocessor support
bytes.title: Turbopack CSS Processing — PostCSS, CSS Modules, and Preprocessor Support Out of the Box
bytes.body:
Turbopack handles CSS processing through its built-in transform pipeline, which integrates PostCSS automatically when a `postcss.config.js` (or `.cjs`) is present at the project root — no additional configuration needed. CSS Modules are natively supported: any file ending in `.module.css` is scoped automatically, with hashed class names generated at build time rather than runtime, giving you faster HMR updates compared to webpack's approach.

For preprocessors, Turbopack supports Sass/SCSS via `sass` package installation — just `npm install sass` and import `.scss` files directly. Less is not currently supported. Notably, Turbopack processes CSS Modules before PostCSS, meaning your `postcss.config.js` plugins (e.g. `autoprefixer`, `tailwindcss`) run on already-scoped output.

One critical difference from webpack: Turbopack does not support `@import` of non-CSS files inside `.css` files using loaders. Keep preprocessor imports within `.scss`/`.sass` files exclusively. Global styles imported in `_app.tsx` or `layout.tsx` are processed through the same pipeline without any special casing.

bytes.topic: Aliasing & Path Resolution — @ aliases, module resolution config
bytes.title: Turbopack Path Resolution — tsconfig Aliases vs. resolveAlias
bytes.body:
Turbopack reads `tsconfig.json` `compilerOptions.paths` natively — no `babel-plugin-module-resolver` or `webpack.resolve.alias` shim needed. Set `baseUrl: "."` alongside your `paths` map and Turbopack picks them up automatically when running `next dev --turbopack`.

For bundler-level remapping — swapping an entire third-party package, e.g. pointing `lodash` to `lodash-es` — use `resolveAlias` inside the `turbopack` key in `next.config.ts`. This key graduated out of `experimental.turbo` in Next.js 15. It operates on the module graph before TypeScript awareness, so it covers JS-only imports that `tsconfig paths` cannot touch.

One critical gotcha: Turbopack resolves `paths` patterns literally at runtime. Aliasing `"@/utils": ["./src/utils"]` will not match an import of `@/utils/format` — you need the wildcard form `"@/utils/*": ["./src/utils/*"]`. TypeScript's language server may autocomplete the import correctly while Turbopack still fails to resolve it, so keep path patterns consistent.

`resolveExtensions` in the same block controls the extension probe order, defaulting to `[".tsx", ".ts", ".jsx", ".js", ".mjs", ".cjs"]`.

bytes.topic: Performance — build speed, caching, parallel processing, incremental builds
bytes.title: Turbopack's Persistent Caching — Why Your Second Build Is Near-Instant
bytes.body:
Turbopack stores build artifacts in a persistent on-disk cache under `.next/cache/turbopack`. Unlike webpack's cache which serializes entire module graphs, Turbopack caches at the granularity of individual task outputs — each transform, resolve, and chunk emission is a discrete node in a demand-driven task graph. When you change a single file, only the subgraph downstream of that file is invalidated and re-executed; everything else is a cache hit.

This is powered by Turbopack's `turbo-tasks` runtime, which tracks fine-grained input/output dependencies automatically. You don't annotate anything — the framework observes which cached values each task reads and rewrites only what changed.

To force a full cache bust during debugging, delete `.next/cache/turbopack` or pass `--no-cache` to your dev command. For CI pipelines, restore this directory between runs using your runner's cache action — Turbopack's cache is fully relocatable and keyed by content hash, not absolute path. Teams on large Next.js 15 monorepos report cold CI builds dropping from 4+ minutes to under 40 seconds after wiring this up.

bytes.topic: Production Optimization — minification, compression (gzip/brotli), dead code elimination
bytes.title: Turbopack Production Optimization — Minification, Compression, and Dead Code Elimination
bytes.body:
Turbopack's production pipeline uses SWC's native Rust minifier instead of Terser — it's enabled automatically with `next build --turbopack` and requires zero config. Unlike webpack, Turbopack builds a fine-grained module graph entirely in Rust, enabling more precise dead code elimination: only exports statically reachable from your entry points survive. Dynamic `import()` boundaries define automatic split points, so lazy routes drop out of the critical bundle without any manual chunking hints.

For compression, Turbopack defers to Next.js's `compress: true` option (gzip via Node's zlib). Brotli isn't emitted natively — offload it to Azure Front Door, Cloudflare, or your CDN. The one silent tree-shaking killer: barrel files (`index.ts` re-exporting everything). Turbopack can't statically analyze non-explicit re-exports, so unused code leaks through. Use `optimizePackageImports` in `next.config.ts` to force granular resolution for heavy libraries. To audit what actually ships, run with `NEXT_TURBOPACK_TRACING=1` and inspect the generated `.next/trace` file via Turbopack's trace viewer.

bytes.topic: Plugin System — how plugins extend the build pipeline
bytes.title: Turbopack Plugin System — Extending the Build Pipeline with Loader Rules
bytes.body:
Turbopack doesn't expose a hook-based plugin API like webpack's Tapable. Instead, it extends the build pipeline through `turbopack.rules` in `next.config.ts` — a file-glob-to-loader mapping that runs webpack-compatible loaders as Turbopack transforms. Each rule targets a file pattern and chains one or more loaders, which Turbopack executes in a Rust-managed async task graph rather than Node.js worker threads — meaning loader output is cached at the task boundary, not per-compilation.

Loaders receive the same `this` context as in webpack (`resourcePath`, `query`, etc.), but hooks like `this.emitFile` and `this.addDependency` have partial support — verify against the Turbopack loader compatibility matrix before migrating. The `as` field normalizes output MIME type so Turbopack's internal module graph correctly classifies the transform result (e.g., treating `.svg` output as `.js`). `resolveAlias` complements rules by intercepting module resolution before the loader pipeline runs, making it the right lever for polyfilling or swapping Node built-ins at build time.

bytes.topic: Vite-specific — native ESM in dev, esbuild pre-bundling, Rollup in prod
bytes.title: Turbopack vs. Vite's Three-Tool Split: Why There's No optimizeDeps
bytes.body:
Turbopack is a direct architectural rejection of Vite's three-tool pipeline: native ESM serving in dev, esbuild for dependency pre-bundling, and Rollup for production. Vite's unbundled ESM dev server is elegant but degrades badly at scale — thousands of individual module requests create a waterfall that bogs down cold starts on large apps. That's why Vite introduced `optimizeDeps.include`: a manual escape hatch to pre-bundle problematic CJS dependencies with esbuild.

Turbopack sidesteps this entirely with demand-driven bundling. When the browser requests a route, Turbopack traces only that route's dependency graph and bundles exactly what's needed — no pre-bundling phase, no `optimizeDeps` hacks. The Turbo engine's incremental cache stores computation results keyed by file content and transform inputs, so subsequent requests for overlapping modules hit the cache at a function-call level of granularity.

Critically, Turbopack uses a single SWC-based transform pipeline in both dev and production. Vite's esbuild-in-dev, Rollup-in-prod split is a known source of behavioral divergence — plugins that work in dev silently break in prod. Turbopack eliminates that entire class of bug by never splitting the pipeline.

bytes.topic: Webpack-specific — loaders vs plugins, module federation
bytes.title: Turbopack — No Plugins, Selective Loaders, and Module Federation's New Shape
bytes.body:
Turbopack collapses webpack's loader/plugin split by design. Plugins don't exist — what webpack plugins did (tree-shaking, chunk splitting, asset hashing) is baked into Turbopack's Rust core and configured via next.config.ts top-level keys, not a plugin array. Loaders survive, but only for file-level transforms. You wire them through `turbopack.rules`, mapping glob patterns to webpack-compatible loaders with an explicit `as` target so Turbopack knows the output module type. Not all loaders are supported — those that tap webpack's compilation lifecycle (html-webpack-plugin, mini-css-extract-plugin) have no equivalent surface.

Module Federation gets its own story. Turbopack ships native MF support — no `ModuleFederationPlugin` import. You declare remotes and exposes inside the `turbopack.modulefederation` key. Turbopack handles the async boundary and shared singleton negotiation at the Rust layer, so the runtime overhead is lower than webpack's JS-based container runtime. The gotcha: `eager` shared modules behave differently — Turbopack enforces async chunks at federation boundaries even when `eager: true` is set, so bootstrapping via a dynamic import wrapper is still required.

bytes.topic: Monorepo Support — workspace packages, shared configs, turborepo integration
bytes.title: Turbopack in a Monorepo — Native Workspace Resolution Without Transpile Hacks
bytes.body:
Turbopack resolves workspace packages by tracing the module graph directly from source — no pre-build step required. Unlike webpack-based setups, you don't need `transpilePackages` for every internal package; Turbopack natively understands package `exports` fields and TypeScript path aliases configured in `tsconfig.json`.

For shared configs (ESLint, tsconfig, Tailwind), reference them via `extends` from a workspace package — Turbopack picks up the inheritance during its incremental compilation pass without needing to watch config files explicitly.

Turborepo integration tightens the loop: in `turbo.json`, mark your `dev` task as `persistent: true` and add `dependsOn: ["^build"]` so shared packages compile before the consuming app starts. Turbopack's file-system watcher operates at the workspace root, meaning a change in `packages/ui` triggers hot module replacement in `apps/web` without manual cache busting.

One gotcha: workspace packages using non-standard or conditional `exports` maps won't resolve automatically. Patch them with `resolveAlias` under the `turbopack` key in `next.config.ts`. Reserve `transpilePackages` only for CJS-only packages that ship without an `exports` field.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — config sharing, extending base configs, CI build caching
bytes.title: Turbopack — Sharing Configs Across a Monorepo and Caching CI Builds
bytes.body:
Turbopack's configuration lives under the `turbopack` key in `next.config.ts`, introduced in Next.js 15. In a monorepo, share a base config by exporting a typed `NextConfig['turbopack']` object from a shared package and spreading it per-app — this keeps loader rules and `resolveAlias` maps DRY across workspaces. To extend, spread the base and override specific keys; `resolveAlias` and `rules` are both plain objects, so a shallow spread is enough for most cases.

For CI, Turbopack's incremental cache lives at `.next/cache/turbopack`. Cache this directory in GitHub Actions using `actions/cache` keyed on a hash of `package-lock.json` and `next.config.ts`. On a cache hit, Turbopack skips recompiling unchanged modules — cold CI builds that took 90s can drop under 10s after the first run. With Turborepo remote caching enabled (`turbo login && turbo link`), `.next/cache/turbopack` artifacts are shared across all CI runners and developer machines via Vercel's content-addressed store, giving you distributed cache hits without any extra configuration.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — build time tracking, bundle size regression alerts in CI
bytes.title: Turbopack — Build Time Tracking and Bundle Size Regression Alerts in CI
bytes.body:
Turbopack exposes build metrics via `--log-level verbose` and structured JSON output when running `next build` with the `TURBOPACK=1` flag. Capture these in CI by piping stdout to a parser that extracts the `compileTime` and per-chunk `size` fields from Turbopack's trace output.

Store baseline metrics as a CI artifact on your main branch. On each PR, compare the new build output against the baseline using a threshold script — flag any chunk exceeding a 10% size increase or build time regression beyond 15 seconds. Turbopack's module graph is deterministic per cache state, so size regressions almost always point to an unintended import or a missing tree-shake boundary.

Use `NEXT_TURBOPACK_TRACING=1` to emit `.trace` files compatible with Perfetto. These files break down time by transform, resolve, and chunk phases — critical for pinpointing which module is bloating your compile time. Pair this with GitHub Actions' `actions/upload-artifact` to persist traces per PR for async review.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .github/workflows/bundle-check.yml
- name: Build with Turbopack tracing
  env:
    TURBOPACK: 1
    NEXT_TURBOPACK_TRACING: 1
  run: next build 2>&1 | tee build-output.txt

- name: Check bundle size regression
  run: |
    node scripts/check-bundle-size.mjs \
      --baseline .bundle-baseline.json \
      --current build-output.txt \
      --threshold 10

- name: Upload Turbopack trace
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: turbopack-trace-${{ github.sha }}
    path: .next/trace
